tokens: And we're going to follow our tracks.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Right behind (meaning west of) the LM.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We'll call it Station 10 prime, and it's about 50 meters to the northwest of the old 10.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (Pause) Dadgummit, now I can't get the...(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I'll take the same set of (Hasselblad) pictures coming back, Tony...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, get your arm, you got...
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: (The same set of pictures that) we took going out to get you a different Sun angle.
This taking pictures from the Rover here is really good.
The (RCU-mounted) camera is just in the right position, Tony; so, hopefully, they will come out.
Okay, off to the right - we're at
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You know, so far on this trip, my personal estimation of Charlie's slope estimates that you can take them and double them.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: My 10 percents never added up to a hundred, John.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Yeah, but you're not exaggerating here, I'll tell you that.
Houston, this is the roughest terrain to be "plains" I ever saw.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: ...we've heard that before on almost every geology trip.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I don't think the photo-geologists get close enough to their subjects.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay, Tony; looking back now at 2 o'clock - and we're heading 140 (at a bearing and range of)
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Palmetto is one of the highest features around, and its rim is really raised.
I can see some large blocks on the north side of it that I couldn't see before.
And I would estimate (they are) 2 meters or so in size.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Light static) Off to the right toward that - which we described going out - now I can see four or five 2-meter-size boulders, whitish in color, with no fillets around them.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I guess we would be off the (North Ray) ejecta blanket here, wouldn't you say, John?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: This probably is right in between the North and South Ray ejecta because there's hardly any pebbles.
It's almost a completely smooth surface, maybe 2 percent, Tony, (is covered) with cobbles up to 5 centimeters.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: As that Sun gets higher, it must be about like looking in zero phase everywhere.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: The visibility gets pretty good at high Sun angles.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: It's still bad going into the Sun...Out of the Sun and into the Sun.
But it's pretty good the way we're going right now.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: You want to take a picture into Palmetto, Charlie?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: If we could get in there, I'd like to.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That's going to be a pretty steep slope though.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We'll go up there, and do a 360 (Rover) pan.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I'll do use one frame setting.
It'll be a little off on exposure, but they'll see what it looks like.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (To Tony) (We've) developed a new technique for panning, Houston.
(Turning through) 360 degrees on the Rover and (Charlie) clicking away.
That ought to make stereo for you.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay, we look like we're in an old secondary area now, Tony, at
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I think this is a South Ray ray.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: A big hunk of South Ray.
They're the same rocks and everything (that they saw during the EVA-2 traverse).
(To Tony) And we're traversing it right now.
It's a big rough, big old blanket full of South Ray material.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Look at those big rocks off to the west there, John, up on the...You probably can't see them, but there are about ten (that) stand out starkly against...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: ...the sky line on the far ridge.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Yeah, there's one of those black rocks like we got out of South Ray.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Houston, we've talked about that one when we passed by it the first time.
That's the same rock that we saw on the (Palmetto) rim.
That's the same class of rock, I would think, that was that big House Rock.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: ...we reduced the (Station 13) magnetometer reading now, and it's 313 gammas down, which is by far the strongest reading we've seen on the Moon.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (Reacting to Tony's news about the magnetometer) Golly!
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I wonder what that...(Stops to listen to Tony's request that they turn off the DAC)
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: (Grunts as he reaches up) Dumb thing.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (To Tony) Well, I wouldn't be surprised but what a big impact crater (like North Ray) wouldn't change your magnetic field some.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Tony, my impression right now could be proved totally wrong on the sample analysis, but...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Are we up on the rim of Palmetto?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: No, we got a long way to go.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Dot's that white spot up on the top there, John.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Ah, is that...(To Houston) I see the same characteristics in South Ray rocks as North Ray rocks, being the black and the white.
The streaks up the side of the craters are basically the same.
But (with) the North Ray you got to guess at it a little bit more; and that might be totally what my guess is.
It could be wrong, but I kinda think they're at least a similar rock.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Pause) So crazy (a suggestion) they didn't even answer me.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: It was just mind boggling, as Freddo says here.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay, I think you...I think we could make it up over there, John, if you broke (meaning "turned") right here, but I guess you want to follow the old (outbound) tracks.
We were pretty close to the rim there for one time.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Let's not do any R&D (Research and Development).
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: But, Tony, if we sample one of these very fresh craters with the indurated regolith that to me look like they're maybe the freshest thing around, can y'all date that just from the regolith?
The glassy shards or whatever is in there?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I was just asking a question about whether you could date a fresh crater with the indurated regolith?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Ah, we'll work on an answer on that.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: There's a lot of them around the (Palmetto) rim, and I'd like to at least pick up some of that and see what y'all could do with it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That sounds like a good idea.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: ...got a definite "maybe" from the Backroom there.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: And, Tony, we're looking into End Crater, and it's a blocky crater.
There are blocks inside of it, and there's some on the rim, half-meter size.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And you were asking about a stop (to collect a sample from a small, fresh crater)...
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: And maybe 10 percent of it...(Correcting himself) Naw, let's make it...(Stops to listen)
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We want to go on to (Station) 10-Prime.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Naw, we weren't talking about a stop.
I was just describing End Crater there.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: How do you like this (ride), Charlie?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: (Pause) Boy, not having that fender really sprays it up there over you, doesn't it?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And could we have an amp-hour reading...(Correcting himself) An amp reading ...
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Tony, occasionally, when we hit a big bump...(Stops to listen) 40.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: When we hit a bump, Tony, and bounce down, that rear wheel that lost the fender showers dirt all over the front of us, and that's what gets the LCRU and the (TV) camera so dirty.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (To John) Man, I'll give you...You earned your driver's license on this thing, John, I tell you.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You know, it takes just about any terrain.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Sure is climbing a slope right now, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We're only doing...Got a v-max (that is, John is applying maximum throttle), and we're only doing 8 clicks.
So you know this has got to be steep.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We're pulling a little over 40 amps, Tony.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Don't anybody tell Ken (Mattingly, the Command Module Pilot) how dirty we are.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Yeah, he won't let us in the hatch.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Just passing End Crater; and the rocks appear to be the same as we sampled, in texture.
They may be not as shocked as the ones up on the rim.
I could grab you one in about a minute.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Let's go over and look at the (Palmetto) rim, John, here.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Incidentally, we were able to track you in the Rover on the way out with the PSE and they are able to see you now on the way back with the active seismic geophones.
We'd like to be sure that you are in the same tracks you were going out.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: We're gonna to drive over to the rim of Palmetto, which is a pretty good sight.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And, we see some blocks on the inner rim; but nothing that really appears to be outcrop.
But it's really a deep crater, Tony.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We can't see the bottom, and we're right on the rim.
It must be a 100 meters or so deep.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I thought we were going to do a 1...3...180...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Pause) Okay, starting now: click - click - click - click - click - click.
It might not be completely overlapped, but I think it will be good enough.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And the rim here is cobbly.
I wouldn't say it's blocky, but it's cobbly.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I think we've been averaging 11 kilometers (per hour), Houston.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: (To John) Look at that view back towards South Ray, isn't that spectacular?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: ...at your tracks, could you tell when you picked up the softer regolith?
On the tracks that you came out?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Softer regolith coming out from the LM?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We're keeping up on it, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: ...mentioned up on North Ray that you only went in...
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: ...an eighth of an inch or so.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (To Tony) Naw, the regolith does change character right past the LM, and it's a lot less blocky, you know?
I think this ray (material) right here...
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I think this is probably...Yeah, it's probably ray (material).
(Pause) Either that or it came out of Palmetto.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (To Tony) This would have been a good choice for a walking traverse (in the event of a Rover failure).
See over there in the west, (pause) over there in the far rim over there?
Don't you see something that looks like...Down in that ledge down there.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: There are two spots over there that might be outcrop, Tony.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Well, that's probably a secondary from...That's probably a big block clot (sic) from...
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: There's one further around, though on the southwest rim, John, that sticks out like it's eroded away from underneath it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That has a hint of bedrock.
But it's...And that's about on the southwest rim, about 20 percent down from the upper rim of Palmetto, Tony.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: But it's just one little isolated block.
And I don't really think it means much.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'm getting them; as fast as I can pull the trigger.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We thought we'd be able to see the rim (means the LM) from here, Tony, but you can't do it...(Correcting himself) I mean (see) the LM, but you can't do it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Well, we're all holding on to our chairs.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (To Houston) Between Gator and Palmetto...(Stops to listen) We almost hit a great big rock, but old Percy (Precision), here, avoided it.
(A LM bearing of) 194, John, takes us right out our (outbound) tracks.

(To Houston) Between Gator and Palmetto, Tony, there's a swale - a depression - that runs east-west that is apparently more cratered and (has) fresher craters there than what we've been driving on between Palmetto and North Ray.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I just finished my 2 pounds of potassium (meaning the potassium-fortified orange drink in his
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I don't know whether I'm driving or sloshing.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Don't let's go unstable with a fuel slosh mode.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Laughing) My fuel slosh problem is getting to be something fierce.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And the Command Module just did their plane change burn, and it's a good burn.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Ah, the old (garbled) (Pause) Man; we are showered (by dust thrown up by the fenderless rear-wheel).
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (To Houston) How many gammas did you say that LSM (Lunar Surface Magnetometer) was, Tony?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: How many times higher than that...That's 10 times higher than what the Apollo 12 guys got, isn't it?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: No, it's not that much greater.
It's about 100 more than they got on Apollo 14.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Well, the magnetic field of the Moon, in places, is a lot more than anybody ever believed it would be.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: From lunar orbit, it only looks like...From high lunar orbit, it only looks like 2 or 3 gamma, at most.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: John, that looks like an endogenic crater right over there to me.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: It has no rim to it...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I see what you're saying there.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Looks like a sink hole; a big doodlebug hole.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And that's, Tony, at our 2 o'clock, and we're presently at 0.9 at 198.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We know exactly which one you're talking about.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: It's only about 100 meters or so from us.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Is it on top of a little dome?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: And you left the ground again.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: No, we almost...We spun out on my side.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Tony, this crater is about, I'd say, at least 200 meters across, has no rim, and no blocks associated with it, except for rays (meaning that the rocks he can see in the crater are South Ray ejecta).
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (To John) Can we do a three (sixty)...(Stops to listen)
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: A little bit further right, John.
Now you can go back (on the original track).
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Now there is a fresh crater with glass right in the bottom of that little fresh crater right back there.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And 190 at 0.7; 198 at 0.7 is the crater that Charlie's talking about.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: (To Houston) And that's not on the map, either.

I guess my opinion of this place is that on our traverse maps, the rimless features - the very old, subdued, rimless craters - they just don't even show up (on the photo map), and they're just not there, hardly.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I mean unless they've got a raised rim, they don't make it.
They don't show up in photography, apparently.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Tony, that sink hole or whatever it was - (that is, the) subdued crater we passed back there - is really deep.
I'm surprised...I really don't have a map right here with me, but it was probably 20...(re-estimating) 40 meters deep or so.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We've got it on the map here.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: We couldn't see the bottom of it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: This is an absolutely great suspension system, Houston.
You should see some of the things we've run through, and this baby just bounces right out and keeps right on going.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We ought to see the old beauty (meaning the LM) when we top the rise here.
(Pause) John just ran over a basketball-size rock with the right wheel and just...There she is, John!
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're about on top of a ridge maybe 30 meters (in elevation) above (the LM), and that Nav system has us pointed right at the Lunar Module.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: It (meaning the Nav system range reading)'s 0.4.
We're about 20 meters up...(correcting himself) 30 meters up above it, Tony.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Here's another real fresh one (meaning a small, fresh crater, probably with glass in the bottom).
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: ...an edge of the ray in the neighborhood of 50 meters north of the ALSEP area, that would be a good place to pick Station 10-Prime.
(Pause) Our photo shows the edge of the ray in there.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: That would be a good place to look for it from, Charlie, right up here.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Does he want to a ray?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Pick the of a ray, 50 meters north of an ALSEP site...(chuckles, correcting himself) of ALSEP site.
(Pause) (Laughing) Tony, I just don't...You can't hardly tell where one...They're not as distinct...The gradational pattern is just too gradual...
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: ...(lost under Tony) especially when ...(Stops to listen)
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: ...And just pick a site 50 meters north (of the ALSEP) and we'll call that 10-Prime.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Tony, we must be out of (DAC) battery power because this camera - this DAC - stopped running...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: ...with about 50 percent of the mag left.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Pause) I guess our calculations were right.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Let me get a picture of that.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I can't believe that big hole there.
I just can't believe it; right behind her.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Let me get another picture of old Orion.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You know that might be an end of a ray right there.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That might be just due to the downslope (motion of regolith off the ridge) though.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Fifty meters...Where do you want to park this thing, Houston?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: That would be over this little ridge here.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (What) we're looking for in the sampling here (are) those vesicular basalts that you both described in the area.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: That might be one right over there, John.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: What heading do you want to park on, Houston?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: ...with the other double core and deep core.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (To Charlie, not having gotten an answer from Tony) Park on a heading of 180?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And that (meaning the LM)'s a beautiful sight.
(Pause) And we're just about directly north of the ALSEP.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Wait a minute, and I'll give you the Rover readings when I dust things off.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're 180, 188, 11.1, 0.1, 30, 120, off-scale low, off-scale low on the amp; batt temps...Looks like we had a failure on batt temp 1.
Left front (motor temperature) is 225; 210 Right front (Static as John switches the TV on) 225.
transcript.speaker: Duke
